' The best personal brander today is Oprah Winfrey - she's a billion dollar

it. That doesn't mean copying her personality, of course, just the way she bonds

The late Dr. Atkins is another exarnple of a powerful personal brand -	a brand he got by doggedly touting his philosophy in the face of constant mockery
and the diet industry actively working against him. The lesson there is simple: If
you have a method or philosophy that you know works, don't let anything stop you
from seliing it. Not Srl1ls making fun of you. Not losers who hang out in forums all
day mocking you. And not any politics in your industry. IJse your daily emails to
give yourself a personal brand and a platform to tell the world about it. This is
probably the #1 most underused aspect of email marketing these days.

influenced by it? (3) the ability to attract or generate revenue (personal revenue or

. People are attracted to a real hurna¡r being they can connect with - this
is one of many reasons why you can be a newbie, not have the best product/

Something daily emails that are personality based and based in honesty (not

How to tell how strong your personal brand is - one of the big litmus tests
is by how much your competition and peers are annoyed by you or by how much
they hate your guts. Think polarization. Guys like Bilt Clinton, Donald Trump,
Thomas Kinkaid (in the artist community), Henry Ford, Dennis Rodman, etc.
L]suall)¡ the more hated )¡ou are the better you're personal brand is. Another litmus
test is if your brand repels as much as it attracts. Rush Limbaugh, for example,
says 500/o of his audience can't stand him. Same with Howard Stern and pretty
much any presidential candidate. Your emails, if done the way I teach in these
newsletters at least, will do this for you by default. You will have people stick
aroundjust to see how much more you can piss them off Sometimes, they become
great buyers. But, not because of your product, but your brand... bottom line: If
your brand doesn't cause a"I cart't stand that guy!" reaction in certain people,
chances are your brand isn't strong enough to turn on the customers you do want.
How to evaluate your current personal brand - do informal market
research: Go to your friends, family, clients, customers, co-workers, etc and simply
ask what do they think about you. What image pops in their head the second they
hear your name? What is their gut reaction to you as a person? When they hear
your name what words and attributes do they think of? What positive things? And,
yes, what NEGAIñlE things? Start with that information and proceed
accordingly. If you have a strong brand you'll get a lot of specific feedback, if you
don't you'll get a lot of vague answers. Of course, with emails you can do this any
time you want. Simply send an email out and ask. Yes, you'll get a lot of fluff and
nonsense and trolls, probably (if your brand is strong at all you should get a troll or
two). Tell them you want honest, open feedback, good or bad. Even better have
them be able to do it in an anon),Ialous way. Take what's useful and honest and
start running with it. Peter Montoya says he's never had a client do that and not be
transformed because of it. For example, I've always been the anti-social introverted
guy who hated going out every night and it used to hold me back when I cared

Out Of Their Minds Until They Buy
One of my favorite "guilty pleasure" moües is called "Blue Velvet" by a director
named Daüd Lynch. It basically is about a guy who comes home from college to care for
his dad who just had a heart attack. While walking in a field he finds a severed ear. And,
from there, he gets sucked into a bízarre local thug's world and, well, I won't spoil it for
you. The plot isn't what's important. It's the finding aL ear. Or the bad guy (brilliantly
played by the late Dennis Hopper) as he puts a mask on and breathes like a psycho while
he torments a woman whose son he has kidnapped. Or how Lynch has a character
walking out of shadows into light like some kind of weird noir film. And, also, the other
random weird stuff Lynch fills his moües and TV shows with so that there is practically
always something off-key going on grabbing your attention. It keeps sucking your
attention span in as it wanders off and forces the üewer to ask "what is that..." and have
to stick around to get the answers. He did this especially well in his cult hit show "Twin
Peaks" - whether it be a chick with an eye patch, a dancing midget in the main
character's dreams, weird music, a room service waiter who sees the main character
bleeding to death in his hotel room and gives him a thumbs up as he lays there, alady
who waiks around carrying a iog everywhere she goes, and the list goes on. Every scene,
every character, every line of dialog (for the most part) has something weird, out of the
ordinary and random going on. And it works like crazy to keep your attention. In Daüd
Lynch's case he's the kLrd of "disturb, offend, or mystify" moviemaker. And, as an email
marketer, you too should h^y to be more disturbing, offending, and mystifying. There are
many ways to do this. I talk a bit about the bizarre subject line in the "Email Players
Playbook", but that's just one way to do it. Another is pepper your emails with unusual
language and switch around words in phrases, or paraphrase popular phrases. I did it
earlier in this issue'uvhen I said, "rolling and rocking" instead of "rocking and rolling."
Sometimes I'11 say, "plenty of fish to bake" instead of "plenty of fish to fry.', or ,,Lots of
ways to skin this chicken" instead of "lots of ways to skin this cat" or just making up

Your mission:
Watch some Daüd Lynch moües. Watch "Blue Velvet" anda few episodes of

Yes, It IS Your Prospect's Fault,
Stop Lying To Thern
Several months ago we had a contest on Facebook to help promote my Copy
Slacker product. Basically, people had to use my system to write a sales letter and the
winner won a fatjuicy pnze. Ary*uy, during that contest I noticed a few of the sales
letters blatantly sayng word-for-word "it's not your fault" in the copy. I see it all over the
Internet in other places, too. And, it's almost become a staple of what people think should
be in sales letters. But you know what? I üsagree. In fact, in most cases I think it is
fundamentally dishonest to say (the actual words) it's not their fault. It[o, chances are
whatever problem they are t.yrng to solve (and yes, I know there are exceptions) it IS their
fault. And if you lie to them in a sales letter b)' sa)'ing word-for-word it's not their fault,
pou are doing them a disservice and to an)¡ skeptics reading the ad, probably
making yourself look dishonest. Why? Because copy has to be rooted in honesty. Yes, I
know various goo-roos (name drop all you want, I don't care) say never tell them it's their
fault. And, I'm not saying you have to come out and say, "It IS your fault!" But, don't lie
to them in a sales letter by telling them it's not their fault in the cases where you and the
prospect know damn well it is their fault they are where they are, even if their
raionalízation hamster is spinning the facts somewhere else.
True story:
At the time I was writing an email to sell a blood sugar supplement- I could have
went with the "it's not your fault your blood suga^r is at 500 from eatingpasta, carbs, and

Soon after that, he was in a state where he was "aware" but couldn't move, speak, or talk,

üdeo for their song "One". The doctors said even if Patrice lived, he'd be in that "locked
in" state the rest of his life -just existing like a starfish or amoeba, aware of everyone

a family and loved ones behind who depended on him, etc. And then, I segued into how
if the reader wants to see some ways help support his own blood suga! including away

No*, you can obviously do what you want. You can kiss customer ass and pander
to their laziness and tell them it's not their fault. Or, you can show them in firll HD color
and surround sound the consequences of not taking responsibility for their dilemma (by
demonstrating it, not lecturing them) the way I did in that email above. In my experience,
the latter way works way better for getting long term repeat buyers, being able to
charge higher prices, and having less headaches and customer service issues.
